Name Change: ASP rebranded to World Surf League (WSL) in 2015
Management Transition: Association of Surfing Pros (ASP) took over in 1983, ASP acquired by ZoSea (2013)
CEO Appointments: Sophie Goldschmidt appointed CEO in 2017, Erik Logan appointed CEO in 2020
Social Media Presence: Over 6.5 million Facebook fans as of December 2017
Viewership Statistics: 28 million hours of digital video content consumed in 2017, WSL ranked third most watched sport online in the US after NFL and NBA
Digital Broadcast Rights: Exclusive $30 million deal with Facebook for two years starting in 2018.
Predecessor Organization: International Surfing Federation (now International Surfing Association)
Current Status: WSL remains a prominent governing body for professional surfers, showcasing talent in progressive formats with a strong digital presence and significant social media following.

Notable Changes/Cancellations: National finals moved to Newcastle in 2017, broadcasted live on Fox Sports
Event cancelled in 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic. Traditionally, winner awarded prestigious 'Bell' trophy. "Hells Bells" by AC/DC played every morning before competition.
2020 event cancelled due to COVID-19, unique final leg of Triple Crown of Surfing and WSL Championship Tour (excluding 2020)
Unique Aspects/Participants: Top 34 WSL surfers and 32 walk-on trial surfers from around the world

Notable Changes/Cancellations: Replaced by the Billabong Pro in 1985
Founders/Organizers: Duke Kahanamoku, Greg Noll, Fred Hemmings
Significant Participants: Jeff Hakman, Greg Noll, Clyde Aikau, Eddie Aikau

Other Activities: Men's and Women's surfing events, volleyball, skateboarding, skimboarding, 5k Oceanfront run, swimsuit competition

Notable Competitors: Bonga Perkins, Jamie Sterling, Garrett McNamara, Raimana Van Bastolaer, Buzzy Kerbox, Chris Mauro, Kainoa McGee

Historical Significance: Heart of Australian surfing in the late 50s and early 60s
Certification: Association of Surfing Professionals Longboard rated event
Surfboard Innovations: Shorter boards and transitional designs influenced by Kevin Platt, George Greenough, and Trevor Hewston
Wave Quality: Long peeling waves with perfect barrel sections

Organizers: Honolulu City Parks Department, North Shore Bodysurfing Club

Previous Name: Quiksilver in Memory of Eddie Aikau
Key Requirements: Open-ocean swells minimum height, selection of 28 invitees, unique wave face conditions

Location: Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa
Nationalities Represented: United States, Hawaii, Brazil, South Africa

Historical Significance: Initiated as the world's richest surfing event in 1985

Historical Name Changes: West Coast Surfing Championship (1959), United States Surfing Championships (1964), OP Pro (1982)
Achievements: Largest surfing competition, adds $21.5 million to local economy
Controversies: 2013 riot, disturbance resulting in property damage
